analogy: Think of "Structural Organisation in Animals" through the chapter's own opening images: STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79 In the preceding chapters you came across a large variety of organisms, both unicellular and multicellular, of the animal kingdom. In unicellular organisms, all functions like digestion, respiration and reproduction are performed by a single cell. In the complex body... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Structural Organisation in Animals is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that sTRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79 In the preceding chapters you came across a large variety of organisms, both unicellular and multicellular, of the animal kingdom.

The text explains that in unicellular organisms, all functions like digestion, respiration and reproduction are performed by a single cell.

The text explains that in the complex body of multicellular animals the same basic functions are carried out by different groups of cells in a well organised manner.

The text explains that the body of a simple organism like Hydra is made of different types of cells and the number of cells in each type can be in thousands.

The text explains that the human body is composed of billions of cells to perform various functions.

The text explains that how do these cells in the body work together?

The text explains that as you have already learnt in your earlier classes, in multicellular animals, a group of similar cells alongwith intercellular substances perform a specific function.

The text explains that you may be surprised to know that all complex animals consist of only four basic types of tissues.

The text explains that these tissues are organised in specific proportion and pattern to form an organ like stomach, lung, heart and kidney.

The text explains that when two or more organs perform a common function by their physical and/or chemical interaction, they together form organ system, e.g., digestive system, respiratory system, etc.

The text explains that cells, tissues, organs and organ systems split up the work in a way that exhibits division of labour and contribute to the survival of the body as a whole.

The text explains that 7.1 ORGAN AND ORGAN SYSTEM The basic tissues as you have learnt in earlier classes, organise to form organs which in turn associate to form organ systems in the multicellular organisms.

The text explains that such an organisation is essential for more efficient and better coordinated activities of millions of cells constituting an organism.

The text explains that each STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS CHAPTER 7 7.1 Organ and Organ System 7.2 Frogs

The text explains that 80 BIOLOGY organ in our body is made of one or more type of tissues.

The text explains that for example, our heart consists of all the four types of tissues, i.e., epithelial, connective, muscular and neural.

The text explains that they do not have constant body temperature i.e., their body temperature varies with the temperature of the environment.

The text explains that the main digestive glands are liver and pancreas.

The text explains that it can respire in water through skin and through lungs on land.

The text explains that circulatory system is closed with single circulation.

The text explains that nervous system is organised into central, peripheral and autonomic.

The text explains that the organs of urinogenital system are kidneys and urinogenital ducts, which open into the cloaca.

The text explains that the male reproductive organ is a pair of testes.

The text explains that the female reproductive organ is a pair of ovaries.

The text explains that the fertilisation and development are external.

The text explains that the eggs hatch into tadpoles, which metamorphose into frogs.

The text explains that draw a neat diagram of digestive system of frog.

Another source point is that sTRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79 In the preceding chapters you came across a large variety of organisms, both unicellular and multicellular, of the animal kingdom.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7: Structural Organisation in Animals

prelims_traps: Do not treat STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79 and 7.1 ORGAN AND ORGAN SYSTEM as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Structural Organisation in Animals; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Structural Organisation in Animals through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION IN ANIMALS 79 and 7.1 ORGAN AND ORGAN SYSTEM to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
